content.title: Stress, Strain, and Elastic Modulus
content.definition: Material properties describing load-deformation behavior of bone, tendon, and implant metals.
content.summary: Stiff implants shield bone (stress shielding); tendon viscoelasticity affects repair loading.

framing.relevance: Foundation for fracture fixation, arthroplasty stability, and implant design.

content.title: Moment Arms and Lever Mechanics
content.definition: Force multiplication at joints and effect of offset on implant and fixation loading.
content.summary: Hip abductor moment arm affects Trendelenburg gait after arthroplasty.

framing.relevance: Foundation for fracture fixation, arthroplasty stability, and implant design.

content.title: Joint Reaction Forces
content.definition: Forces across hip, knee, and shoulder during gait and activity estimated by free-body analysis.
content.summary: Peak hip joint reaction exceeds three times body weight in normal gait.

framing.relevance: Foundation for fracture fixation, arthroplasty stability, and implant design.

content.title: Fixation Construct Biomechanics
content.definition: Load sharing between implant and bone in plates, nails, and external fixators.
content.summary: Bridge plating reduces bone strain; lag screws convert tensile to compressive forces at fracture site.

framing.relevance: Foundation for fracture fixation, arthroplasty stability, and implant design.

content.title: Implant Materials and Tribology — Core Principles
content.definition: Properties of metals, polymers, and ceramics used in orthopaedic implants, including wear mechanisms at bearing interfaces.
content.summary: Modulus mismatch drives stress shielding. Cross-linked polyethylene, ceramic-on-ceramic, and metal-on-metal bearings differ in wear and osteolysis risk.

content.definition: Systematic study of human locomotion phases, joint kinematics, and kinetics during the gait cycle.
content.summary: Stance and swing phases divide gait. Hip, knee, and ankle moments peak at distinct phases. Pathologic gait patterns localize neuromuscular and structural deficits.

content.title: Compartment Syndrome Pathophysiology
content.definition: Elevated intracompartmental pressure reducing capillary perfusion below critical threshold.
content.summary: Ischemia-reperfusion causes irreversible muscle and nerve damage within hours.

framing.relevance: Time-critical diagnosis in trauma and reperfusion injury.

content.title: Compartment Syndrome Clinical Diagnosis
content.definition: Pain out of proportion, pain with passive stretch, paresthesias, pallor, pulselessness, paralysis.
content.summary: Clinical diagnosis in awake patients; do not wait for compartment pressure if exam is convincing.

framing.relevance: Time-critical diagnosis in trauma and reperfusion injury.

content.title: Compartment Pressure Measurement
content.definition: Intracompartmental pressure monitoring when clinical exam is unreliable (sedation, intubation, children).
content.summary: Delta pressure (diastolic BP minus compartment pressure) ≤30 mmHg indicates syndrome.

framing.relevance: Time-critical diagnosis in trauma and reperfusion injury.

content.title: Fasciotomy Technique and Complications
content.definition: Two-incision leg fasciotomy and forearm volar-dorsal release with wound management.
content.summary: Delayed fasciotomy risks Volkmann contracture; skin grafting often required for wound closure.

framing.relevance: Time-critical diagnosis in trauma and reperfusion injury.

content.title: Volkmann Ischemic Contracture
content.definition: Fixed flexion deformity of forearm and hand after untreated forearm compartment syndrome.
content.summary: Prevention is emergent fasciotomy; late treatment includes tendon lengthening and free muscle transfer.

framing.relevance: Time-critical diagnosis in trauma and reperfusion injury.

content.title: Descriptive Fracture Terminology
content.definition: Standard terms for fracture location, pattern, displacement, angulation, and associated soft-tissue injury.
content.summary: Consistent nomenclature enables communication, research, and treatment planning across teams.

framing.relevance: Foundation for all trauma subspecialty management.

content.title: AO/OTA Fracture Classification
content.definition: Alphanumeric system classifying fractures by bone segment, location, and pattern complexity.
content.summary: AO/OTA provides universal long-bone and periarticular classification; higher numbers indicate increasing complexity.

framing.relevance: Foundation for all trauma subspecialty management.

content.title: Regional Classification Systems
content.definition: Anatomic-region schemes including Neer (proximal humerus), Garden (femoral neck), Gustilo (open fractures), and Schatzker (tibial plateau).
content.summary: Regional classifications guide operative indication and prognosis within specific anatomic areas.

framing.relevance: Foundation for all trauma subspecialty management.

content.title: Gustilo-Anderson Open Fracture Grading
content.definition: Soft-tissue injury grading for open fractures predicting infection risk and guiding antibiotic duration.
content.summary: Type IIIB requires soft-tissue coverage; type IIIC includes arterial injury requiring repair.

framing.relevance: Foundation for all trauma subspecialty management.

content.title: Classification in Treatment Planning and Prognosis
content.definition: Using classification to select operative versus nonoperative management and estimate complication rates.
content.summary: Classification alone does not dictate treatment — patient factors and soft-tissue status modify algorithms.

framing.title_in_context: Classification in Treatment Planning and Prognosis
framing.relevance: Foundation for all trauma subspecialty management.

hierarchy_location.subtopic: Classification in Treatment Planning and Prognosis

content.title: Absolute vs Relative Stability
content.definition: Fixation constructs provide either absolute stability (anatomic reduction, primary bone healing) or relative stability (micro-motion, secondary healing with callus).
content.summary: Lag screw and compression plating aim for absolute stability; bridge plating and intramedullary nails permit controlled motion for secondary healing.

framing.relevance: Core trauma knowledge integrating biomechanics and bone healing biology.

source_references.source: StatPearls — Fracture Reduction and Fixation Principles

content.title: Reduction Techniques — Open and Closed
content.definition: Methods to restore length, alignment, and rotation including closed manipulation, traction, and open direct reduction.
content.summary: Malreduction in rotation or length causes functional deficit. Indirect reduction preserves soft-tissue attachments when possible.

framing.relevance: Core trauma knowledge integrating biomechanics and bone healing biology.

source_references.source: StatPearls — Fracture Reduction and Fixation Principles

content.title: Plate and Screw Fixation
content.definition: Application of plates and screws including lag screw technique, neutralization, compression, and bridge plating patterns.
content.summary: Plate length and screw density distribute stress. Periosteum devascularization risk increases with extensive stripping.

framing.relevance: Core trauma knowledge integrating biomechanics and bone healing biology.

source_references.source: StatPearls — Fracture Reduction and Fixation Principles

content.title: Locking Plate Biomechanics
content.definition: Fixed-angle locking screw-plate constructs provide angular stability in osteoporotic or comminuted bone.
content.summary: Locking plates function as internal fixators; avoid compression on poor-quality bone.

framing.relevance: Core trauma knowledge integrating biomechanics and bone healing biology.

source_references.source: StatPearls — Fracture Reduction and Fixation Principles

content.title: Intramedullary Nailing and External Fixation
content.definition: Load-sharing intramedullary implants and external fixators for diaphyseal fractures, polytrauma, and soft-tissue compromise.
content.summary: Reamed nailing is standard for closed femoral shaft fractures. External fixation temporizes in damage-control and open-fracture settings.

framing.relevance: Core trauma knowledge integrating biomechanics and bone healing biology.

source_references.source: StatPearls — Fracture Reduction and Fixation Principles

content.title: Damage-Control External Fixation
content.definition: Temporary spanning external fixation to stabilize long-bone fractures before physiologic resuscitation and definitive fixation.
content.summary: Pin-site care and construct planning allow conversion to IM nail or plate when patient stabilizes.

framing.relevance: Core trauma knowledge integrating biomechanics and bone healing biology.

source_references.source: StatPearls — Fracture Reduction and Fixation Principles

content.title: Bone Grafts — Autograft and Allograft
content.definition: Autograft provides osteogenic cells, osteoinductive factors, and osteoconductive scaffold; allograft supplies osteoconduction without living cells.
content.summary: Iliac crest autograft remains gold standard for biologic augmentation; allograft avoids donor-site morbidity with slower incorporation.

framing.relevance: Core trauma knowledge integrating biomechanics and bone healing biology.

content.definition: Demineralized bone matrix, bone morphogenetic proteins, and synthetic ceramics as adjuncts for defect filling and fusion enhancement.
content.summary: rhBMP-2 indications include certain spinal fusions and tibial nonunions; cost, swelling, and heterotopic bone are considerations.

content.title: Open Fracture Initial Management
content.definition: Emergency assessment, tetanus prophylaxis, culture-directed antibiotics, and sterile dressing for open fractures.
content.summary: Early IV antibiotics within one hour reduce infection; avoid repeated wound probing in the field.

framing.relevance: High-yield ABOS trauma topic with time-sensitive management.

source_references.source: StatPearls — Open Fractures and Mangled Extremity

content.title: Gustilo Classification and Antibiotic Protocols
content.definition: Grading open fractures and selecting antibiotic duration and coverage based on wound severity.
content.summary: First-generation cephalosporin is standard; add aminoglycoside for type III; penicillin for farm injuries.

framing.relevance: High-yield ABOS trauma topic with time-sensitive management.

source_references.source: StatPearls — Open Fractures and Mangled Extremity

content.title: Surgical Debridement and Wound Management
content.definition: Timely débridement of devitalized tissue, irrigation, and stabilization to convert contaminated wounds to clean surgical fields.
content.summary: Serial débridement may be required; vacuum-assisted closure bridges to definitive coverage.

framing.relevance: High-yield ABOS trauma topic with time-sensitive management.

source_references.source: StatPearls — Open Fractures and Mangled Extremity

content.title: Negative Pressure Wound Therapy in Open Fractures
content.definition: VAC/NPWT as bridge to flap coverage in high-grade open fractures.
content.summary: Reduces edema and bacterial load while preparing wound bed for definitive closure.

content.title: Mangled Extremity Severity Score (MESS)
content.definition: Scoring system combining injury energy, limb ischemia, shock, and age to aid salvage versus amputation decisions.
content.summary: MESS ≥7 suggests amputation may be appropriate; clinical judgment and patient preference remain paramount.

framing.relevance: High-yield ABOS trauma topic with time-sensitive management.

source_references.source: StatPearls — Open Fractures and Mangled Extremity

content.title: Polytrauma Assessment and ISS
content.definition: Injury Severity Score and physiologic markers guiding orthopaedic timing in multiply injured patients.
content.summary: ISS >40 and lactate elevation favor damage-control strategy.

framing.relevance: ABOS multiple trauma section; integrates with ATLS principles.

source_references.source: StatPearls — Polytrauma and Damage Control Orthopaedics

content.title: Damage Control Orthopaedic Principles
content.definition: Temporary stabilization with external fixation before definitive fixation after resuscitation.
content.summary: Limit operative time and blood loss in first 24 hours; plan second-look and definitive surgery.

framing.relevance: ABOS multiple trauma section; integrates with ATLS principles.

source_references.source: StatPearls — Polytrauma and Damage Control Orthopaedics

content.title: Fat Embolism and Second-Hit Phenomenon
content.definition: Systemic inflammatory response after long-bone fixation in unstable patients causing ARDS and coagulopathy.
content.summary: Early IM nailing of femur in stable patients is safe; delay when physiologically deranged.

framing.relevance: ABOS multiple trauma section; integrates with ATLS principles.

source_references.source: StatPearls — Polytrauma and Damage Control Orthopaedics

content.title: Definitive Fixation Timing
content.definition: Conversion from external fixation to IM nail or plate when lactate normalized and coagulopathy corrected.
content.summary: Window often 5–10 days; pin-site infection risk increases with prolonged external fixation.

framing.relevance: ABOS multiple trauma section; integrates with ATLS principles.

source_references.source: StatPearls — Polytrauma and Damage Control Orthopaedics

content.title: Clavicle, Acromioclavicular, and Sternoclavicular Injuries — Core Principles

content.summary: Midshaft clavicle, AC separations (Rockwood), and SC dislocations including posterior SC mediastinal risk. Brachial plexus injury may accompany clavicle trauma.

content.definition: Kocher-Langenbeck, ilioinguinal, and Stoppa approaches matched to fracture pattern.
content.summary: Approach selection balances exposure with sciatic and vascular risk.

content.definition: Joint degeneration following malreduction or articular cartilage injury.

content.title: THA Indications and Contraindications
content.definition: End-stage hip arthritis, osteonecrosis, and selected fracture indications with medical optimization.
content.summary: Active infection and severe neurologic disease are contraindications.

framing.relevance: Core ABOS adult reconstruction topic.

content.title: THA Surgical Approaches
content.definition: Posterior, direct anterior, anterolateral, and dual-mobility considerations.
content.summary: Posterior approach has dislocation risk; anterior may reduce muscle damage with learning curve.

framing.relevance: Core ABOS adult reconstruction topic.

content.title: THA Component Fixation
content.definition: Cemented versus cementless fixation, porous coating, and dual-mobility bearings.
content.summary: Cementless fixation relies on initial scratch fit and biologic ingrowth.

framing.relevance: Core ABOS adult reconstruction topic.

content.title: THA Complications
content.definition: Dislocation, leg-length inequality, nerve injury, infection, and periprosthetic fracture.
content.summary: Posterior soft-tissue repair and patient education reduce dislocation rate.

framing.relevance: Core ABOS adult reconstruction topic.

content.title: THA Dislocation Management
content.definition: Closed reduction, abduction brace, and revision for recurrent instability.
content.summary: Component malposition and soft-tissue insufficiency are revision indications.

framing.relevance: Core ABOS adult reconstruction topic.

content.title: TKA Indications and Alignment Goals
content.definition: End-stage tricompartmental knee arthritis with mechanical axis restoration targets.
content.summary: Neutral mechanical axis within 3 degrees is traditional goal; kinematic alignment is alternative philosophy.

framing.relevance: Among the most common elective orthopaedic procedures.

content.title: TKA Component Design and Soft-Tissue Balance
content.definition: Femoral and tibial component sizing, polyethylene thickness, and gap balancing in flexion and extension.
content.summary: Posterior-stabilized versus cruciate-retaining designs depend on PCL integrity and surgeon preference.

framing.title_in_context: TKA Component Design and Soft-Tissue Balance
framing.relevance: Among the most common elective orthopaedic procedures.

hierarchy_location.subtopic: TKA Component Design and Soft-Tissue Balance

content.title: TKA Surgical Technique
content.definition: Measured resection versus gap technique, patellar resurfacing decisions, and tourniquet use.
content.summary: Patellar maltracking causes anterior knee pain; component rotation affects flexion gap.

framing.relevance: Among the most common elective orthopaedic procedures.

content.title: TKA Complications
content.definition: Stiffness, instability, patellar complications, infection, and periprosthetic fracture.
content.summary: Manipulation under anesthesia for early stiffness; revision for instability from malrotation.

framing.relevance: Among the most common elective orthopaedic procedures.

content.definition: Replacement of a single articular compartment or hemiarthroplasty of the femoral head for localized joint disease.
content.summary: UKA requires intact ACL and single-compartment disease. Hip hemiarthroplasty is used for displaced femoral neck fractures in selected elderly patients.

content.title: Meniscal Anatomy and Tear Patterns
content.definition: Red-red, red-white, and white-white zones; longitudinal, bucket-handle, radial, and root tears.
content.summary: Peripheral tears in vascular zone heal with repair; white-white often partial meniscectomy.

framing.relevance: Cartilage/meniscus ABOS sports section.

content.title: Meniscal Tear Clinical Presentation
content.definition: Joint line tenderness, effusion, locking, and McMurray test.
content.summary: Bucket-handle tear causes mechanical block to extension.

framing.relevance: Cartilage/meniscus ABOS sports section.

content.title: Meniscal Repair Indications
content.definition: Vertical longitudinal tears in young patients with stable knee and adequate tissue quality.
content.summary: All-inside, inside-out, and outside-in techniques; protect with brace and limited flexion.

framing.relevance: Cartilage/meniscus ABOS sports section.

content.title: Meniscectomy and Long-Term Outcomes
content.definition: Partial versus total meniscectomy and accelerated OA risk.

framing.relevance: Cartilage/meniscus ABOS sports section.

content.title: Anterior Shoulder Instability

content.title: Posterior and Multidirectional Instability

framing.relevance: High-yield paediatric hip emergency.

content.title: SCFE Diagnosis
content.definition: Frog-leg lateral radiograph showing slip of femoral epiphysis relative to metaphysis.
content.summary: Klein line fails to intersect epiphysis on AP view; bilateral in 20–40%.

content.title: SCFE In-Situ Pinning
content.definition: Single or double screw fixation without further slip; avoid forceful reduction.
content.summary: One screw in center-center position; prophylactic pinning of contralateral hip in high risk.

framing.relevance: High-yield paediatric hip emergency.

content.title: SCFE Complications
content.definition: Avascular necrosis, chondrolysis, femoroacetabular impingement, and osteoarthritis.
content.summary: AVN risk highest in unstable slips with attempted reduction.

content.title: Charcot Clinical Diagnosis
content.definition: Warm, swollen, red foot with minimal pain and intact pulses distinguishing from cellulitis.

content.title: Charcot Nonoperative Management
content.definition: Total contact cast and protected weight-bearing until consolidation.
